candidate_problems.text: How can 'structurally underdetermined at the token level but overdetermined at the system level' be formalized in a way that yields testable necessary or sufficient conditions for invariant learning? The conjecture is compelling but currently qualitative: it needs a precise measure of when hidden causes become identifiable or optimization-favored under next-token loss, and how this differs from mere mutual information or compression.

What distinguishes latent variables that genuinely track real domain invariants from internal features that only mimic them instrumentally within the training distribution? The open tension is whether prediction pressure selects truth-tracking structure, causal structure, or just reusable predictive shortcuts, especially under distribution shift or when multiple incompatible latent decompositions achieve similar loss.

How much of the effect is due to the data-generating structure versus the learner's architecture, objective, and optimization dynamics? Even if the predictive ecology contains indispensable hidden causes, it remains unresolved whether standard next-token training reliably discovers them, or whether induction heads, attention limits, context windows, regularization, and scale determine whether the model converges to invariant-based representations or shallow token-circulation solutions.
